biggest: Choose the biggest of a given set of objects in terms of your own common sense
heaviest: Choose the heaviest of a given set of objects in terms of your own common sense
fits: Answer whether an object could fit in a given container using your own common sense
interact: Given 2 states an object can be in (as defined in ontology), choose which one is easiest for the average person to use
can_do: Choose which objects can perform a randomly selected function
can_do_size: find functions such that the members can be differentiated by size, then fill in the blanks from that narrowed list
can_do_shape: find functions such that the members can be differentiated by shape/volume/length, then fill in the blanks from that narrowed list
can_do_char: find functions such that the members can be differentiated by secondary characteristics, then fill in the blanks from that narrowed list
can_do_goal: find functions such that the members can be differentiated by specific goals, then fill in the blanks from that narrowed list
difference: Choose two objects with the same function, then create answers with similarities, differences, and falsehoods for the LLM to choose between
diff_criteria: Choose two objects with the same function and a way to differentiate them, then generate answers that either describe the differentiation, a similarity, or another way the objects are different
use_as: Select two objects with the same function, then decide if the first is an adequate substitute for the second
is_a: Find major hypernyms in each function, mark them, then decide whether to use a hypernym or not during generation
types_of: give a list of objects where some are a sub-category of a main object, e.g. choose the trucks has answers 18 wheeler and pickup
injury: give a list of objects and an injury event and choose which object would cause the worst injury
danger: give a list of objects and an dangerous event, then choose which object would be the most dangerous
damage_to_obj: given a large object, choose which object from a list could cause the most damage to the large object
explain_use: create basic step by step instructions for how to use an object associated with safety, earthquakes, or search and rescue. Select 2 of the steps and ask which one comes first
equip_used: Given an earthquake or search and relief subevent, choose the object that is used in that event
equip_in_task: given an disaster relief and/or earthquake related object, write answer choices for what that object does for a particular subtask
instruct: choose a category that only describes 1 instruction (navigation, identification, travelling, etc) from a list of 5
followup: Randomly select an instruction then write a plausible follow up question for it
obj_loc: describe where an object is typically found
objs_in_loc: given a place, what object would you find?
secondary_use: Given a task, choose the object that complete said task but was not originally made to do so
often_use: Answer how often a typical person would use an object.
know_use: Answer how well a typical person knows how to use a piece of disaster related equipment
prepare: Choose the object required for preparation for an aspect of an earthquake disaster

fits: Randomly select an object from the ontology
can_do: Randomly select a function from the ontology, then turn it into english in as few words as possible
can_do_size: For each function in the ontology, examine its members and use common sense to determine if they are easily differentiated by relative size of an object. For example, go.02 INSTRUMENT objects can be split into "bigger than couch" and "smaller than couch" fairly intuitively. Mark each function that fits those criteria, then randomly select a function and turn it into english
can_do_shape: For each function in the ontology, examine its members and use common sense to determine if they are easily differentiated by shape, volume, or length. For example, contain.01 ARG0 can be differentiated by round vs square, and read.01 ARG1 can be differentiated by thickness. Mark each function that can be differentiated, then randomly select one of the marked functions and turn it into english
can_do_char: For each function in the ontology, examine its members and use common sense to determine if they are easily differentiated by a secondary physical characteristic beyond shape and size. For example, go.02 INSTRUMENT can be differentiated by holding one person or holding multiple people, and shelter.01 ARG1 has items that only shelter from above, not the sides. Mark each function that can be differentiated, then randomly select one of the marked functions and turn it into english
can_do_goal: For each function in the ontology, examine its members and use common sense to determine if they are easily differentiated by a more specific goal of usage. For example, climb.01 ARG1 can be differentiated by "getting to a 2nd story window", and go.02 INSTRUMENT can be differentiated by "go to remote island". Mark each function that can be differentiated, then randomly select one of the marked functions and turn it into english
difference: Randomly select 2 objects from the same function. If you think they are too similar to have meaningful differences, resample. Otherwise, these will be the objects used.
diff_criteria: Randomly select 2 objects from the same function. If you think they are too similar to have meaningful differences, resample. Otherwise, these will be the objects used.
use_as: Randomly select 2 objects from the same function group.
is_a: For each function, determine if there's a hypernym relationship between any members and record it. Then, when filling in the blanks, flip a coin to determine if you will use a hypernym relationship. If yes, then randomly sample a hypernym relationship from the list. If no, randomly sample 2 objects with the same function.
types_of: Choose a hypernym that has several hyponyms that do not include the name of the hypernym
injury: Choose an event where an injury could occur. The main events of interest are objects falling on someone, hitting someone, tripping someone, and cutting someone.
danger: Choose a dangerous event. The main events of interest are objects falling, hitting, collapsing, and trapping.
damage_to_obj: Choose a large object (most likely a vehicle or a building) from the vocabulary list
explain_use: choose a disaster-related object that requires multiple steps to use
equip_used: Create a task related to earthquakes and/or search and rescue based on expert knowledge
equip_in_task: choose an earthquake and/or search and rescue related object based on expert descriptions.
instruct: This should be a descriptive and somewhat general category for what an instruction is about
followup: randomly select an instruction from the list of instructions Taylor made
obj_loc: Randomly select an object from the vocabulary
objs_in_loc: Select a location from the vocabulary (likely an occupy.01 ARG1)
secondary_use: Choose a secondary task. These include breaking windows, being a lever, barricading a door, etc.
often_use: Choose a random object from the vocabulary list
know_use: Randomly select a piece of disaster equipment provided by the expert

fits: Randomly select an object from the ontology with the function contain.01 ARG0
can_do_size: For each function marked as usable for this template, examine its members and determine at least 1 object that is bigger than 1+ member(s) of the function and smaller than 1+ member(s) of the function. You can use the same size-comparison-object for different functions so long as it works for the above criteria. When the function is randomly selected, its corresponding object is always used to fill in the second blank. Finally, randomly select if the answers are "bigger" or "smaller" than the object chosen in the template.
can_do_shape: For each function marked as usable for this template, examine its members and determine a general shape, volume, or length that 1+ objects in the function groups possess. You can use the same shape entity for different functions so long as it works for the above criteria. When the function is randomly selected, its corresponding shape is always used to fill in the second blank.
can_do_char: For each function marked as usable for this template, examine its members and determine as many relevant secondary characteristics as possible such that 1+ objects in the function possess said characteristic. You can use the same characteristic for different functions so long as it works for the above criteria. Clearly describe this use case in plain english with as few words as necessary. When a function is randomly selected, randomly select one of the function's secondary characteristics to fill in the second blank.
can_do_goal: For each function marked as usable for this template, examine its members and determine as many relevant goals as possible such that 1+ objects in the function can fulfill the goal. You can use goal for different functions so long as it works for the above criteria. Clearly describe this use case in plain english with as few words as necessary. When a function is randomly selected, randomly select one of the function's specific goals to fill in the second blank.
diff_criteria: Choose a somewhat general way to differentiate the objects. Common ones include color, texture, material, size, shape, use case, and breakability, but you may choose a different way than the members of the above list.
explain_use: Create a simple set of ordered instructions, then randomly select 2 steps. Label them A and B.
equip_in_task: This should be the corresponding sub-task to the object

Template name: Answer Choices Description (note: all answers should have 5 choices unless otherwise specified)
biggest: Randomly select 5 objects from the ontology, then choose the biggest in terms of size/volume, not in terms of weight. If there isn't a clear-cut answer, take another random sample until there is
heaviest: Randomly select 5 objects from the ontology, then choose the heaviest/densest not the biggest. If there isn't a clear-cut answer, take another random sample until there is
fits: Use your own common sense to determine if the object could fit in that type of container, or in any of its subtypes (e.g. a car can fit in a shipping crate). The answer choices should be "can fit" and "cannot fit"
can_do: Randomly select a term that has the randomly selected function to be the correct. Then, randomly sample 4 other terms from the ontology to be the other answer choices. If one of the randomly selected answer choices also has the same role as the correct answer, mark it as a correct answer as well.
can_do_size: Select 1 answer from the function that is bigger than the object, 1 that is smaller, one that can be either bigger or smaller, and 2 terms with a different function
can_do_shape: Select 1 answer from the function that has the shape/volume, 2 that do not, and 2 terms with a different function function
can_do_char: Select 1 answer from the function that have the given secondary characteristic, 2 answers from the function that do not have the secondary characteristic, and 2 terms with a different function.
can_do_goal: Select 1 answer from the function that can fulfill the given goal, 2 answers from the function that do not fulfill the goal, and 2 terms with a different function.
difference: Write 1 difference between the objects, 2 similarities, and 2 falsehoods about one or both of the objects
diff_criteria: Write 1 correct answer of how the objects are differentiated, one incorrect answer for how the objects are differentiated using the criteria given (e.g. a baseball is purple and a basketball is blue, if the criteria was color) 1-2 incorrect answers that are true about the object differences but not for the criteria (e.g. a baseball is smaller than a basketball, if the criteria is color), and 1-2 answers that are false assertions about the object that have nothing to do with the criteria (e.g. a baseball is used for hockey and a basketball is used for basketball)
use_as: Use common sense judgement to determine if the first object is an adequate substitute for the second object for the typical use cases of the objects. For instance, you can use a bowl as a plate since both can hold food, but you can't use a plate as a bowl since a plate can't hold liquid. Answer "x can be used as y", or "x cannot be used as y"
is_a: Use common sense judgement to determine if the first object is an example of the second object. For instance, a box is a container, but a plate is not a bowl. Answer "x is a(n) y", or "x is not a(n) y"
types_of: 1 answer should be a hyponym, 2 answers should be objects related to the hyponym that are not hyponyms themselves, and 2 answers should be random objects, or a hypernym to the hypernym (e.g. vehicle to truck) and 1 random object
injury: Randomly generate 5 objects, then choose which one would cause the most severe injury. If none seem to be a risk for the given injury event, randomly generate more objects until you get 1+ objects that do.
danger: Randomly generate 5 objects, then choose which one would be most dangerous for a group of people to experience. If none seem to be a risk for the given dangerous event, randomly generate more objects until you get 1+ objects that do.
damage_to_obj: Randomly generate 5 objects, then choose which one would cause the most damage to the large object. If none seem to be able to cause any damage, randomly generate more objects until you get 1+ objects that do.
explain_use: the answer choices are either Step A comes first or Step B comes first. Use your original instructions to determine which is the case
equip_used: 1 answer choice should be the object used for the task, 2 answer choices should be objects used for different tasks in the domain, and 2 answer choices should be random objects.
equip_in_task: The answer choices should be brief descriptions of what the object might do in the subtask. 1 answer choice should be the correct choice, 2 answer choices should be what different objects do in this or a related subtask, and 2 answer choices should be false attributes of the object.
instruct: Create 1 simple instruction that falls under the category. Randomly select the remaining 4 answer choices from the list of simple instructions Taylor made.
followup: Write a plausible follow up question to the randomly chosen instruction. The other 4 answer choices should be randomly selected from the list of follow-up questions Taylor made.
obj_loc: 1 answer choice should be a fairly specific place where the object is typically found. The other 4 choices should be places where the object is not usually found.
objs_in_loc: 1 answer choice should be an object found in that space. 2 answer choices should be objects found in related places, and 2 anser choices can be randomly selected objets from the vocabulary that won't be found in the space.
secondary_use: 1 answer choice should be an object that can do the given task but was not built to do so. The other answer choices should be objects that cannot accomplish the task.
often_use: The answer choices are very often, often, somewhat often, somewhat rarely, and rarely
know_use: The answer choices are very well, well, somewhat well, not well, and no idea
